config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. Society matters a lot, but I do not think it should routinely outrank individual rights, because that creates too much room for abuse by governments or majorities. In my view, rights are the guardrails that keep society fair, especially for minorities and people with less power. From my perspective, the best society is one that protects the common good without sacrificing basic individual freedoms.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because in the United States we have seen how easily "society" can become a justification for trampling minority rights and individual liberty. Public safety and the common good matter, but they should not be a blank check for government or the majority to override fundamental rights whenever it is convenient. I would only put society first in narrow cases like preventing direct harm, and even then the burden should be very high. A healthy society is one that protects the individual first and asks citizens to contribute to the common good without losing core freedoms.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “the interests of society” can be used to silence dissent, pressure minorities, or excuse heavy-handed government action. Society does matter, but it is not a magic word that should automatically override individual rights, especially when those rights protect people who are already vulnerable or unpopular. The common good is strongest when it is built on clear limits, so public goals do not become an excuse for abuse. In my view, a fair society protects basic rights first and then asks people to contribute to the collective good within those boundaries.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement, because in the United States we have learned that “society first” can quickly become a slogan for majoritarian overreach and government abuse. Public safety, public health, and the common good absolutely matter, but they should not automatically outrank individual rights, especially when those rights protect unpopular minorities or dissenters. I do not buy the idea that society gets priority just because it is called the common good; the burden should be on the state to prove that any limit on rights is narrow, necessary, and truly justified. In my view, a free society is strongest when individual rights are the default and collective interests have to earn their exception.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement: in Indonesia, “society first” has too often been used to pressure minorities, silence criticism, or justify state overreach. The common good is important, but it should not automatically outrank individual rights, because rights are what protect people when the majority or authorities get it wrong. I also do not accept the idea that society should get priority just because it sounds noble; any limit on rights has to be narrow, necessary, and accountable. A healthy society is built by protecting basic freedoms first, then pursuing collective goals within those limits.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because in the United States we have too many examples of “society first” turning into majority rule with no real limits. Public safety and the common good matter, but they do not deserve automatic priority over individual rights, especially when those rights protect minorities, dissenters, or unpopular speech. I do not think the label “social interest” should let government or the majority skip the hard work of proving a restriction is necessary, narrow, and justified. A free society works best when individual rights are the default and collective goals have to earn exceptions.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
